content: This is the clearest read on the 301 review I have seen. We pulled our last twelve months of entries like you said and the top three line items were not what I expected.
Ray, that surprise is the whole point of running the report. Now you know where to look at sourcing first. Happy to walk through the alternatives when you are ready.
The labor value content piece is exactly where we got tripped up last year. We had the RVC math cold and still could not back up the LVC. Lesson learned.
Teresa, you are not alone on that. Build the supplier affidavits into your onboarding and it stops being a fire drill. Glad it landed.
We got hit with a liquidated damages claim on a late ISF last quarter. Five thousand dollars for a filing that was a day late. Wish I had read this first.
Karen, that one stings because it is so avoidable. Set the filing trigger off the booking, not the sail date, and you build in a buffer. Call me if you want to set that up.
The hold and call the carrier line is going on a sticky note over my desk. Learned that one the hard way.
Most of our small parcel apparel comes in under de minimis. If that threshold drops our landed cost math changes overnight. Glad you flagged it early.
David, run the model now while it is calm. If it changes you want the number ready, not a scramble. Send me your volume and I will help you stress test it.
We assumed 232 only touched the raw coil we buy. Turns out a couple of our finished components are on the derivatives list. This post saved us an audit headache.
Anthony, the derivatives list catches almost everybody at least once. Check it every time the rules update, not just when you start a new product.
The supply chain map back to raw material is the part our suppliers resist. Any tips on getting cotton origin documentation that actually holds up?
Susan, put it in the purchase terms so it is a condition of the order, not a favor. Suppliers who will not document origin are telling you something. We can draft that language for you.
Doing this annual code recheck as a habit now because of the mentorship sessions. Found two subheadings that changed this year on my own entries.
Jordan, that is exactly the instinct I want you to build. Catching it yourself beats CBP catching it every single time. Good work.
One of our clients got a deposit rate jump from eight percent to over a hundred in a review. Nobody saw it coming until the entry posted. Brutal.
Frank, that is why the producer specific rate matters so much. The all others rate hides how bad a specific case can get. Track the case docket and there are no surprises.
We export a good chunk of what we import and never filed drawback because it looked like a paperwork nightmare. After this we ran the numbers. It is very much worth it.
Michael, the recordkeeping is real but so is the refund. With your volume the five year lookback alone is worth the setup. Let us scope it.
We got caught by the lock closure two winters ago and had to reroute. Expensive lesson. We mark the closure dates on the calendar now.
Gloria, the close sneaks up on people every year. Booking to the lock date instead of the delivery date is the fix. Glad you are ahead of it now.
The pick one and get ahead of it advice is the most useful thing I read all month. For our clients it is de minimis. Starting there.
Carla, smart call. You cannot boil the ocean. Pick the one that touches your goods and go deep. Bring questions to the next session.
We got a USMCA verification letter last fall and the deadline was tighter than we expected. Having the origin file ready in advance was the only thing that saved us.
Linda, that is the whole game. The letter is a deadline, not a request. You did the hard part before it arrived. Well handled.
The boring and it works line is the truth. The mentees always want the exotic stuff and it is almost always classification and valuation basics that bite people.
Ryan, exactly. Glamour does not pay penalties, homework prevents them. We should build a session around the prior disclosure angle for the spring cohort.
Looking forward to the spring cohort. The classification problems are hard in the best way. Thanks for keeping the blog going too, I read every one.
Devin, glad to have you in the cohort. Keep bringing the tough ones. The blog stays as long as people keep reading and asking.
Sorting entries by 301 duty paid is such a simple move and I had never thought to do it that way. Did it for a client and the priority list wrote itself.
